data.title: GE Vernova declares $0.50 dividend
data.description:
• GE Vernova (GEV) declares $0.50/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 0.24%
  • Payable April 14; for shareholders of record March 17; ex-div March 17.
  • The company has now announced a dividend of $0.50 for two consecutive quarters.
  • See GEV Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Nvidia sells remaining stake in SoftBank-backed chip designer Arm Holdings
data.description:
• Nvidia (NVDA) has sold its remaining stake in Arm Holdings (ARM), the chip technology firm it attempted but ultimately failed to acquire five years ago.
  • Nvidia (NVDA) unloaded 1.1 million shares, worth about $140 million based on Arm’s closing price Tuesday, according to a regulatory filing. The disposal brings Nvidia’s stake to zero.
  • AI chip leader Nvidia sought to buy Arm (ARM) for $40 billion in 2020, a transaction that would have been the largest deal ever in the semiconductor industry. The proposed takeover drew strong resistance from regulators and customers, ultimately leading to its collapse in February 2022. Arm, which is majority-owned by SoftBank Group (SFTBY) (SFTBF), has since moved ahead with plans for a public listing.

data.title: Tesla escapes California sales suspension over Autopilot marketing
data.description:
The California Department of Motor Vehicles said Tesla (TSLA) has brought its marketing practices into compliance and will avoid a previously threatened 30-day suspension of vehicle sales in the state.

In a statement Tuesday, the agency said Tesla has taken “corrective action” to address concerns related to how it promotes its automated-driving capabilities.

In December, Tesla was given 90 days to come into compliance after a ruling from an administrative judge on the state regulator’s allegations that the company was overstating the capabilities of features marketed as Autopilot and Full Self-Driving.

Since then, the EV maker has discontinued its Autopilot product and increasingly adopted the term “Full Self-Driving (Supervised)” in its advertising, emphasizing that the driver-assistance system is not fully autonomous and requires continuous driver oversight.

data.title: Leslie's reaffirms $1.1B–$1.25B sales guidance as pricing transformation and store optimization advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Leslie's, Inc. (LESL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jason McDonell, CEO, affirmed, "our unwavering commitment to become America's one-stop shop for pool care drives every action we're taking to position Leslie's for a return to sustainable profitable growth." He highlighted the company's comprehensive transformation plan, focusing on customer value and operational efficiency.
  • McDonell announced, "we reaffirmed our full year guidance for net sales at $1.1 billion to $1.25 billion and adjusted EBITDA in a range of $55 million to $75 million." He noted "encouraging momentum with positive comparable store sales in January" and confidence in the pricing transformation initiative, which will move to national implementation for the 2026 pool season.
  • The CEO outlined a nationwide pricing strategy to address a loss of 160,000 residential customers in the prior year, saying, "our pricing strategy is designed to drive traffic, increase conversion rates and build customer loyalty."
  • Significant store optimization progress was detailed, with 80 underperforming locations closed—"approximately 80% of these closures in less than 7 days after we made our announcement last quarter."
  • The CEO described the ongoing distribution center consolidation, SKU reduction efforts, and a restructuring of field operations to a market leadership model, aiming for deeper customer relationships and transaction growth.
  • Jeffrey White, CFO, stated, "Net sales for the first quarter were $147.1 million, largely in line with our internal top line expectations as it aligns to our full year guide. This is compared to $175.2 million in the first quarter of the prior year or a 16% decline."
  • White added, "Gross profit margin for the first quarter was 18.4% versus 27.2% in the prior year period. Approximately 430 basis points of this gross profit decline during the period was due to the onetime noncash impairment charge taken on inventory located within stores that were closed during the period."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed full year guidance for fiscal 2026: "net sales at $1.1 billion to $1.25 billion and adjusted EBITDA in a range of $55 million to $75 million."
  • The pricing investments are expected to "cause an approximate 100 to 150 basis point decline in annual gross profit margins on a year-over-year basis for fiscal 2026."
  • Store optimization is projected to result in an "annual sales impact of approximately $25 million to $35 million," with an "annualized net EBITDA improvement of $4 million to $10 million once fully completed by the end of Q2 2026."
  • Expense reduction initiatives are expected to deliver an additional "$7 million to $12 million of annualized savings with benefits starting to be realized in the second half of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the quarter were reported as $147.1 million.
  • Comparable sales decreased 15.5% in the first quarter compared with the same period in fiscal 2025.
  • Gross profit margin was 18.4%, with a 430 basis point impact from noncash impairment charges on closed store inventory.
  • SG&A decreased $1.7 million to $85.7 million.
  • Net loss for the first quarter was $83 million, with adjusted net loss at $48.7 million compared to $40.7 million in the prior year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter was negative $40.3 million.
  • Inventory at quarter end was $210 million, down from $271 million in the prior year's first quarter.
  • Capital expenditures stood at $4.3 million.
  • Outstanding borrowings on the line of credit were $25 million, and long-term debt was $752 million.

Q&A

  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: Asked about the impact of pricing changes on full-year financials and the company's confidence in guidance. Jason McDonell explained the focus on regular price optimization, moving to an everyday value strategy, and targeting basket building. Jeffrey White emphasized, "the amount of expense cuts that we've been able to achieve is a lot greater than the $7 million to $10 million" net benefit to adjusted EBITDA, with cost savings invested back into pricing changes.
  • Gutman followed up on January's positive sales momentum and whether it reflected an inflection or store closure effects. McDonell responded that a "healthier base" exists post-closures, with continued positive trends in the western U.S. and optimism for the upcoming pool season.
  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Inquired about basket building and lift in unit per transaction or average order value from pricing pilots. McDonell noted variety in tests, positive increases in unit per transaction, and improved conversion rates, reinforcing confidence for the season launch.
  • Matuszewski also asked about long-term store footprint optimization. McDonell stressed strategic proximity and omnichannel integration, while White highlighted both closure and expansion opportunities identified in network studies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment reflected persistent concern about the effects of pricing changes and store closures, with probing on sustainability and quantifiable impacts, suggesting a slightly negative tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks, repeatedly highlighting progress and optimism—"we are reaffirming our full year outlook" and "we remain confident in our strategic direction."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained skeptical, while management shifted from a focus on foundational transformation to greater emphasis on execution and operational progress.
  • During Q&A, management's responses were detailed and generally confident, with no use of defensive trigger phrases or deflection.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net sales and adjusted EBITDA was reaffirmed, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus continued to center on pricing transformation, cost optimization, and store/distribution center rationalization.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on pricing strategy, customer retention, store closures, and the quantifiable impact of transformation initiatives.
  • Key metrics shifted from Q4's year-end performance and impairment charges to Q1's seasonally low sales and ongoing execution of operational changes.
  • Management's tone in Q1 reflected greater confidence in execution, with concrete progress on closures, cost reductions, and marketing rollouts, compared to a more cautious, planning-oriented tone in Q4.
  • Analysts' skepticism persisted regarding the sustainability and effectiveness of cost and pricing initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the "net loss of 160,000 residential customers" in the prior year as a primary challenge, citing pricing as the main driver of churn.
  • Gross margin pressure continues, with "an approximate 100 to 150 basis point decline in annual gross profit margins on a year-over-year basis for fiscal 2026" expected from pricing changes.
  • Inventory optimization and clearance initiatives carry a risk of further margin impact during the transition.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of cost reductions and the long-term impact of store closures on market presence and customer convenience.
  • Management outlined mitigation through targeted marketing, expanded delivery partnerships, and ongoing review of store and distribution networks.

Final Takeaway

Leslie's management highlighted progress on its transformation plan, emphasizing aggressive pricing actions, accelerated store and distribution center optimization, and targeted marketing to win back lapsed customers. The company reaffirmed full-year sales and adjusted EBITDA guidance, with management expressing confidence that cost savings and revamped pricing will support a return to profitable growth as the 2026 pool season approaches.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tesla to integrate Grok AI chatbot into cars across the UK and Europe amid regulatory scrutiny
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) Europe revealed that it will integrate xAI’s Grok AI chatbot into its in-car infotainment systems in the U.K. and eight additional European markets.

Of note, Tesla (TSLA) finished 2025 with weaker European sales, as Chinese automaker BYD (BYDDF) continued to outperform Elon Musk’s EV company.

During a fourth-quarter earnings update, Tesla (TSLA) revealed that it had invested $2 billion into CEO Elon Musk’s xAI. Following that, SpaceX (SPACE), the aerospace and defense powerhouse led by Musk, acquired xAI in an all-stock deal that valued the combined company at $1.25 trillion.

The development, however, comes as xAI's chatbot Grok and X have recently come under scrutiny following the alleged generation of deepfake images by the AI service. On Tuesday, it was reported that Ireland’s data protection regulator opened an inquiry into X amid concerns about sexualized images generated and shared through the Grok AI chatbot.

data.title: Glaukos outlines 2026 sales target of $600M–$620M as iDose TR and Epioxa drive growth momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Glaukos Corporation (GKOS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Glaukos Chairman & CEO Thomas Burns reported record fourth quarter consolidated net sales of $143.1 million, reflecting an increase of 36% year-over-year, and full-year 2025 net sales of $507.4 million, up 32% from 2024. Burns stated, "We are also reaffirming our full year 2026 net sales guidance range of $600 million to $620 million, which implies continued strong year-over-year growth of more than 20% at the midpoint."
  • Key growth drivers highlighted include the ongoing adoption of iDose TR, which generated approximately $45 million in sales during the fourth quarter, and the launch of Epioxa, a newly FDA-approved corneal cross-linking therapy for keratoconus. Burns described iDose TR as "early in its overall adoption curve with significant value yet to be unlocked" and noted the recent FDA approval for unlimited re-administration.
  • Burns emphasized Epioxa's potential, stating, "Interest from the physician community following approval has been very encouraging and reinforces our view that with Epioxa, we are ushering in a new standard of care for keratoconus patients and practitioners."
  • President & COO Joseph Gilliam indicated that the corneal health franchise, including Epioxa and Photrexa, delivered net sales of $24 million in the quarter, and that Epioxa's rollout would involve significant investment in patient awareness, education, and access.
  • CFO Alex Thurman stated, "Our philosophy as a corporation still hasn't changed from what we experienced in 2025, which is we're going to continue to balance our capital investments against our revenues such that we're driving towards cash flow breakeven and potentially some cash flow generation over the course of 2026."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its 2026 net sales guidance range of $600 million to $620 million, driven by iDose TR in the U.S. glaucoma segment and the launch of Epioxa in corneal health. Gilliam detailed, "On the U.S. glaucoma side, we expect embedded in the guidance is growth in the 30% range year-over-year, driven entirely by iDose TR," while corneal health is expected to "grow modestly year-over-year, but with a fair amount of volatility."
  • Management anticipates sequential revenue growth for iDose throughout 2026 and a "strong exit in the fourth quarter" for the corneal health franchise as Epioxa adoption accelerates with the permanent J-code.

Financial Results

  • Glaukos posted record fourth quarter net sales of $143.1 million, with U.S. glaucoma sales reaching $86.4 million, up 53% year-over-year, and iDose TR accounting for approximately $45 million of that. Full-year 2025 consolidated net sales totaled $507.4 million.
  • International glaucoma delivered net sales of $32.8 million, up 18% year-over-year, and corneal health net sales reached $24 million, including $21.4 million from Photrexa.
  • The company surpassed 1,000 employees and broke ground on a new facility in Huntsville, Alabama.

Q&A

  • Tom Stephan, Stifel, asked about payer pushback on Epioxa pricing and Photrexa discontinuation. Gilliam responded that "we've not heard any formal or informal pushback from payers on the pricing dynamics associated with Epioxa."
  • Adam Maeder, Piper Sandler, requested granularity on 2026 guidance. Gilliam explained growth is expected to be high single-digit internationally, 30% in U.S. glaucoma driven by iDose, and modest in corneal health with volatility during Epioxa ramp.
  • Ryan Zimmerman, BTIG, questioned cannibalistic effects between iDose re-administration and TREX. Gilliam replied the goal is "to provide patients and surgeons with as many options as possible," and long-term modeling will depend on clinical outcomes and pricing.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo, inquired about re-administration rates and the halo effect. Gilliam said it's too early to quantify, but expects "considerable opportunity for multiple re-administrations within the same patients over time."
  • Richard Newitter, Truist, asked about co-pay assistance and specialty pharmacy access for Epioxa. Gilliam confirmed a $0 co-pay program for commercially covered lives and increased investment in patient support to optimize access and turnaround time.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on potential pricing headwinds, Epioxa ramp dynamics, and sequential growth cadence, but generally acknowledged strong performance and strategic clarity; tone was neutral to slightly positive.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing ongoing momentum and investment discipline. CEO Burns stated, "Our record fourth quarter and full year results reflect a highly successful year of global execution across our key commercial and development initiatives."
  • Compared to the prior quarter, management expressed greater confidence regarding payer receptivity for Epioxa and sustained iDose TR growth, while analysts continued to probe Epioxa ramp and access risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s 2026 net sales guidance remained unchanged at $600 million to $620 million, but management provided more detailed commentary on segment contributions and quarterly cadence, especially regarding Epioxa’s launch and Photrexa transition.
  • The prior quarter's focus on payer education and potential headwinds from Epioxa pricing shifted to reports of early positive coverage determinations and accelerated site of care network deployment.
  • Management’s tone was more assured on infrastructure readiness and access for Epioxa, while analysts shifted from questions on pricing risk to execution details and sequential revenue flows.
  • Sequential growth in iDose TR sales continued, and Epioxa’s initial deployment exceeded previous expectations for covered population reach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted payer adoption hurdles and initial access limitations for Epioxa, noting, "initial patient access will be gated by our site of care network deployment and typical payer adoption headwinds and hurdles."
  • Transition risk from Photrexa to Epioxa was acknowledged, with volatility expected in corneal health revenues during this period.
  • International glaucoma faces "new competitive product trialing headwinds in some of our major international markets as we progress through 2026."
  • Gilliam indicated that re-administration rates for iDose TR will take time to materialize as a major earnings contributor.

Final Takeaway

Glaukos management emphasized their ongoing transformation into a diversified ophthalmic leader, underpinned by record sales and reaffirmed 2026 guidance. The company’s strategic focus remains on driving adoption of iDose TR and executing a disciplined, infrastructure-intensive launch for Epioxa, with investments in awareness and access expected to unlock further growth and market opportunity in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goosehead targets 10%-19% organic revenue growth and accelerates AI investments for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Goosehead Insurance (GSHD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Miller, President and CEO, highlighted, "For the full year 2025, we grew total revenue 16%, adjusted EBITDA of 14% and delivered an adjusted EBITDA margin of 31%." He noted improvements in client retention, which rose from 84% in the second quarter to 85% in the third, and an acceleration in policies in force, ending the year up 14%. Miller emphasized strategic advancements, stating, "We have seen strong productivity across all 3 distribution networks: corporate, franchise and enterprise sales," and described the company's increased application of AI, proprietary data leverage, and the launch of the Digital Agent 2.0 platform in Texas.
  • Miller also announced, "Gross payments per franchise are up 29% year-over-year," and detailed geographic expansion and consolidation within the franchise network. He underscored the growing importance of technology with the rollout of a mobile app, an AI-powered virtual phone assistant named Lily, and a broader technology roadmap.
  • Mark Jones Jr., COO & CFO, reported, "Our digital agent platform is now live with multiple auto carriers in Texas with true end-to-end binding capability, and we've already seen policies bound with no human involvement." He stated that the enterprise sales and partnerships business nearly doubled headcount to 115 as of year-end and described technology as "the single largest portion of our P&L and rapidly making progress towards our growth and efficiency initiatives."

Outlook

  • Jones Jr. provided 2026 guidance: "Total revenues are expected to grow organically between 10% and 19%. Total written premiums are expected to grow organically between 12% and 20%."
  • Management expects modest margin compression in 2026 due to continued investments in AI, Digital Agent 2.0, and partnerships, but projects that these investments will deliver incremental long-term growth and margin at scale.
  • The company forecasts low double-digit core revenue growth for the first half of 2026 and acceleration in the second half, tied to pricing consistency, continued retention improvements, and scaling of partnerships and new technology.

Financial Results

  • Jones Jr. reported, "Total revenue for the quarter was $105.3 million, up 12% over the previous year quarter and $365.3 million for the full year, growing 16%."
  • Core revenues grew 15% to $78.2 million in the quarter and 16% to $317.9 million for the year, attributed to client retention and new business production.
  • Ancillary revenues, primarily contingent commissions, were $25.3 million for the quarter and $41.1 million for the year. Contingent commissions represented 86 basis points of total written premiums.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $39.2 million, including $2.9 million of incremental strategic investments, and $113.6 million for the year.
  • Cash flow from operations reached $91.8 million, up 28% from the prior year. The company repurchased $81.7 million of Class A shares during 2025 and announced a new $180 million share repurchase authorization.

Q&A

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: Asked about home closing transactions and insurance pricing for 2026 guidance. Jones Jr.: "We're not counting on any improvements in housing in terms of our guidance throughout 2026... the bottom end of the guidance range includes pricing that's generally down." Andersen also questioned regulatory risks; Jones Jr. responded that the company would monitor developments but didn't expect significant near-term impact.
  • Brian Meredith, UBS: Asked about AI and agent disintermediation. Miller stated, "I think it's going to be difficult to disintermediate the clients... the majority of our clients still want some human guidance interaction in the process."
  • Thomas Mcjoynt-Griffith, KBW: Inquired about Digital Agent 2.0 client acquisition and advertising strategy. Jones Jr.: "We're not going to go into a giant advertising campaign to try and drive eyeballs at goosehead.com... our distribution point with the digital agent largely going through the partnership base."
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: Asked about franchise producer trends and consolidation. Jones Jr. noted plans to push the producers per franchise number higher, with demand for new hires described as strong.
  • Katie Sakys, Autonomous Research: Inquired about Net Promoter Score and client experience. Miller explained that NPS remains affected by past price increases, but retention and client satisfaction measures have improved.
  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Sought clarity on investment spending and partnership ramp-up. Jones Jr. confirmed 2026 investment guidance remains at $25 million to $35 million total cash, with $8 million to $11 million impacting the P&L.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a slightly positive yet cautious tone, frequently probing for the impact of AI, margin trajectory, and the durability of the franchise model. There was interest in whether technology investments would yield sustainable margin improvements.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, frequently citing momentum, proprietary data advantages, and scalability. In Q&A, the tone shifted to more measured responses, especially when addressing regulatory uncertainties, investment pacing, and the long-term impact of AI. Miller asserted, "We lead with the home and cross-sell with the auto so I think it makes it even harder for us to get disintermediated in this process."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased focus on execution risks associated with scaling technology and AI, and on the impact of ongoing consolidation within the franchise network.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management guidance shifted to a narrower organic revenue growth range of 10%-19% for 2026, compared to the previous quarter's 11%-22% for 2025, reflecting a more measured outlook.
  • Strategic focus has intensified on scaling enterprise partnerships and accelerating AI-driven initiatives, while franchise network consolidation remains a priority.
  • Analysts shifted their questions from basic growth drivers in Q3 to deeper inquiries about technology, AI monetization, and cost discipline in Q4.
  • Margin commentary evolved, with greater emphasis on near-term compression due to investment, whereas the previous quarter focused more on long-term expansion potential.
  • Key metrics such as client retention, policy growth, and adjusted EBITDA all continued to improve quarter-over-quarter, though management highlighted the transitional impact of investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the impact of regulatory risks, such as potential profitability caps in certain states, and indicated ongoing monitoring of these developments.
  • Margin compression is expected as technology and AI investments accelerate.
  • Franchise network consolidation is recognized as a short-term revenue and producer count headwind, though positioned as value-creating long term.
  • The company is not relying on a housing market rebound in its 2026 guidance, representing a conservative planning stance.
  • Management acknowledged contingent commissions may vary based on catastrophic events and market volatility.

Final Takeaway

Goosehead Insurance concluded 2025 in a position of strength, executing on its strategy of technology-driven growth, franchise network consolidation, and expansion of enterprise sales. Management projects organic revenue growth of 10% to 19% and written premium growth of 12% to 20% for 2026, with near-term margin compression as investments in AI, Digital Agent 2.0, and partnerships accelerate. The company emphasized that improvements in client retention, policy growth, and technology adoption position it for long-term leadership in digital personal lines insurance distribution, while continuing to monitor regulatory risks and ongoing shifts in market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Axcelis anticipates flat 2026 revenue as memory growth offsets declines in power and mature markets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Axcelis Technologies (ACLS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Russell Low highlighted that "we generated solid results in the fourth quarter with revenue of $238 million and non-GAAP earnings per diluted share of $1.49, both exceeding our outlook." He attributed the outperformance to stronger CS&I aftermarket revenue, which also benefited gross margins. CEO Low noted significant progress toward the Veeco merger, stating, "Shareholders of both companies voted in favor of the transaction at our special meetings on February 6." Regulatory clearance has been received in several jurisdictions, with final approval from China pending and closing expected in the second half of 2026.
  • CEO Low explained that mature-node applications, particularly in power and general mature segments, accounted for the majority of system shipments, with bookings improving significantly on a sequential basis. He also introduced the Purion H6 high current ion implanter, emphasizing its advancements in dose repeatability, purity, precision, and productivity.
  • In the power segment, CEO Low observed a slight moderation in silicon carbide shipments and ongoing customer focus on technology transitions. He reported growing interest in Axcelis's high-energy channeling capabilities, particularly for superjunction development.
  • CEO Low stated, "We expect strong long-term demand through the cycles, driven by clear secular trends such as the growing penetration rate of silicon carbide into electric vehicles...and the growing adoption of silicon carbide outside of the automotive sector."
  • In memory, CEO Low said demand improved sequentially for DRAM and HBM, and momentum is expected to extend into 2026. He highlighted a key customer win in North America, broadening Axcelis's presence beyond Korea.
  • CFO James Coogan reported, "Fourth quarter revenue was $238 million, with systems revenue at $156 million and CS&I revenue reaching another quarterly record of $82 million, both above our expectations for the quarter."

Outlook

  • CFO Coogan provided guidance for Q1 2026, stating, "We expect revenue in the first quarter of approximately $195 million." Non-GAAP gross margins are projected at approximately 41%, with non-GAAP operating expenses of around $59 million and adjusted EBITDA of about $26 million. Non-GAAP diluted earnings per share is expected to be approximately $0.71.
  • For full year 2026, CFO Coogan said, "We anticipate overall revenue to be relatively flat compared to 2025 levels." He expects memory market growth to offset declines in the power and general mature markets, with revenue second half weighted.
  • Non-GAAP gross margins for 2026 are estimated in the low to mid-40% range, with a modest tariff impact.

Financial Results

  • Axcelis reported Q4 revenue of $238 million, systems revenue of $156 million, and CS&I revenue of $82 million.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin was 47.3%, above the outlook of 43%. GAAP operating margin was 15.2%, and non-GAAP operating margin was 21.1%. Adjusted EBITDA was $55 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 22.9%.
  • GAAP diluted earnings per share was $1.10; non-GAAP diluted earnings per share was $1.49.
  • Free cash flow in Q4 was negative $9 million, affected by the timing of sales and $5 million in transaction expenses related to the Veeco merger. Full year free cash flow was $107 million.
  • Axcelis repurchased $25 million in shares during Q4 and exited with $557 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Dorsheimer, William Blair: Asked about memory market capacity expansion and DRAM demand. CEO Low said growth is limited by cleanroom space but expects "DRAM significantly up in 2026 compared to 2025, although somewhat of a lower base in '25."
  • Dorsheimer followed up on CS&I silicon carbide upgrades. CEO Low stated, "Only a very small part of our installed base has gone up to 200-millimeter...ample opportunity to continue to provide these really high-value upgrades."
  • Denis Pyatchanin, Needham: Asked about segments driving bookings. CEO Low said bookings matched prior segments, mainly general mature and power, with a bump in memory.
  • Pyatchanin questioned the outlook for power and general mature. CEO Low indicated both segments are "slightly down," with memory picking up the slack as cleanroom space becomes available.
  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: Inquired about memory CapEx cycles and ion implant intensity per 100,000 wafer starts. CEO Low confirmed about $150 million to $200 million in capital for implants in DRAM per 100,000 wafer starts.
  • Craig Ellis, B. Riley: Asked about mature foundry utilization rates and China demand intensity. CEO Low highlighted high utilization rates in China and ongoing demand for equipment to meet self-sufficiency goals. CFO Coogan said, "We see it to be relatively flat to down slightly" for China in 2026.
  • Jack Egan, Charter Equity: Asked about power and general mature guidance. CEO Low cited careful capacity additions in China and rising utilization rates outside China. Egan also queried memory order cycle dynamics, with CFO Coogan clarifying that customer forecasts are typically multi-quarter, supporting confidence in memory recovery.
  • Duksan Jang, BofA: Asked about Q1 guidance components and 2026 second-half revenue weighting. CFO Coogan explained the Q1 guide reflects both pull-in and pushout effects, and memory is expected to drive the second-half weighting.
  • David Duley, Steelhead: Asked about memory growth percentages and historical memory system revenue. CFO Coogan did not offer a specific 2026 percentage but expects healthy growth from a low 2025 base. CEO Low and CFO Coogan anticipate acceleration in 2027.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was predominantly constructive yet probing, with focus on memory momentum, power and general mature weakness, and China demand. Skepticism emerged around the sustainability of CS&I upgrades and timing of a broader recovery.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic wins and long-term opportunities, but responses in Q&A were more measured, especially regarding power and general mature segments, and included cautious language such as "slightly down" and "relatively flat."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted to a more positive outlook on memory, while management’s tone became more confident about memory but remained cautious on power and general mature recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management in Q4 guided for flat overall 2026 revenue, compared to Q3’s outlook of flattish Q4 and Q1 2026 revenues, now specifying memory as the growth driver offsetting declines in power and general mature markets.
  • In Q4, management reported record CS&I revenue and highlighted the launch of Purion H6, whereas Q3 focused on new product announcements and ongoing strategic initiatives.
  • Q4’s Q&A included more detailed discussion of memory’s role and cleanroom constraints, while Q3 analysts focused on China demand and power segments. Management’s confidence in memory has increased, while its stance on power and general mature recovery remains cautious.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted muted near-term demand for silicon carbide and power markets as customers absorb existing capacity, with memory growth constrained by cleanroom space.
  • Tariff impacts are expected to modestly affect margins in 2026.
  • Q1 revenue and gross margin are projected to decline sequentially due to less favorable mix, lower CS&I revenue, and tariff impacts.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of CS&I growth and the timing of recovery in power and general mature segments.

Final Takeaway

Axcelis Technologies delivered strong Q4 results, driven by record CS&I aftermarket revenue and strategic wins in the memory segment. Management anticipates overall 2026 revenue will be flat compared to 2025, with growth in memory, particularly DRAM, offsetting softness in power and general mature markets. The company remains focused on disciplined execution, the rollout of new products like the Purion H6, and integration planning for the pending Veeco merger, while maintaining a solid balance sheet and strong cash position to support future opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Palo Alto Networks targets $11.3B revenue and 53% NGS ARR growth for FY 2026 as AI and identity security drive strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Palo Alto Networks (PANW) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Nikesh Arora stated the company "delivered a strong Q2 fueled by robust demand for cybersecurity and continued execution against our platformization strategy," highlighting NGS ARR up 28% organically and revenue growth of 15%, excluding the impact of Chronosphere. He emphasized the closing of CyberArk and Chronosphere acquisitions, noting, "Both companies continue to deliver record numbers in their most recent quarters, and we look forward to building on the momentum as we hit the ground running on our integration plans."
  • Arora described a market shift, saying, "Large enterprises are moving beyond experimentation and beginning to integrate foundational models into real workflows... the central question that organizations face is shifting from capability to control." He asserted, "Security must operate in real time at the critical control points where decisions are made across network, endpoint, cloud, browser and identity."
  • Strategic wins included a global automotive leader selecting Palo Alto Networks for a $50 million deal and a global technology supplier for over $40 million, both driven by platformization and SASE/XSIAM adoption.
  • SASE ARR surpassed $1.5 billion, growing 40% year-on-year, and software firewall ARR grew 25%. Prisma Browser reached over 1,500 customers and sold 2 million additional licenses in Q2. Prisma AIRS, launched recently, tripled its customer count to over 100 in the quarter.
  • Arora announced the intent to acquire Koi to "enhance our endpoint capabilities within XTR 2.0, while also becoming an integrated part of our universal AI security platform."
  • Chronosphere contributed approximately $200 million in ARR, with a multiyear nine-figure expansion deal signed with a leading AI model provider post-acquisition.
  • The CyberArk acquisition was completed, and Arora outlined, "We're building a next-generational identity security platform that protects across humans, machines and AI agents."
  • CFO Dipak Golechha stated, "Our strong Q2 results reflect the consistent execution of our platformization strategy, coupled with a robust demand environment. The increasing adoption of our platforms is most evident in our next-generation security ARR, which grew 33% to $6.33 billion."

Outlook

  • For Q3 2026, the company expects NGS ARR in the range of $7.94 billion to $7.96 billion, revenue of $2.941 billion to $2.945 billion, and diluted non-GAAP EPS of $0.78 to $0.80.
  • For the full fiscal year 2026, guidance is for NGS ARR of $8.52 billion to $8.62 billion, revenue of $11.28 billion to $11.31 billion, operating margins of 28.5% to 29%, diluted non-GAAP EPS of $3.65 to $3.70, and adjusted free cash flow margin of 37%.
  • Golechha commented, "We believe this disciplined approach to integration reinforces our confidence in delivering sustained growth and operating leverage, enabling us to achieve our target of 40% free cash flow margin by fiscal 2028."

Financial Results

  • NGS ARR (including $200 million from Chronosphere) reached $6.33 billion, with organic growth at 28% year-over-year.
  • Remaining performance obligation (RPO) grew 23% to $16.0 billion.
  • Total revenue was $2.59 billion, up 15%. Product revenue was up 22%, with 45% of product revenue from software form factors over the trailing 12 months.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 76.1%. Product gross margin was 78.2%, services segment gross margin was 75.6%.
  • Operating margin was 30.3%. Diluted non-GAAP EPS was $1.03. Adjusted free cash flow was $502 million for Q2.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $7.9 billion, reflecting the $2.6 billion cash consideration for the Chronosphere acquisition.

Q&A

  • Robbie Owens, Piper Sandler: Asked about the existential nature of the AI shift versus the cloud era and whether M&A remains the primary lever. CEO Arora: Explained the company's position, stating consolidation and platformization are accelerating, "we did our best number of platformizations this quarter, and we've ever done, barring the Q4, which is seasonally strong."
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Queried the risk of LLMs replacing SIEM tools. Arora: "LLMs are a net positive and additive to our capability... until they get to 99%, 99.9% accuracy are not a threat to delivering security."
  • Saket Kalia, Barclays: Asked about joint pipeline opportunities with CyberArk and ARR contribution. Arora: "We already have had CyberArk reps coming us to have an opportunity for Palo Alto's products in an account... I think the opportunity is real."
  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: Asked about SASE business reacceleration. Lee Klarich: Highlighted shift from point product to comprehensive platform needs, "delivering this as a platform is we can bridge how we apply network security... all in a very consistent way."
  • Joshua Tilton, Wolfe Research: Questioned the impact of AI agent adoption on network traffic and demand for security suite. Arora: "It's too early to tell... the challenge right now is consolidating that traffic."
  • John DiFucci, Guggenheim: Asked when AI will drive visible revenue growth. Arora: "It's early days... our number of customers with Prisma AIRS is kind of following the same trajectory as XSIAM."
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: Asked about making CyberArk more accessible. Klarich: Discussed modernization and integration plans, "ideas for how we can leverage integrations between CyberArk and, for example, Prisma Browser."
  • Adam Tindle, Raymond James: Queried Chronosphere's expansion deal and NGS ARR trends. Arora: Noted Chronosphere's scalability and cost advantages, "they are displacing another vendor in that space."
  • Adam Borg, Stifel: Asked about quantum opportunity. Arora: Outlined integration of Venafi and new capabilities, "we have tremendous feedback from them."
  • Gregg Moskowitz, Mizuho: Questioned management focus amid large acquisitions. Arora: Detailed months-long planning and integration, "this is our thirty second or thirty third acquisition... we have a lot of lessons from prior acquisitions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on integration risks, AI's impact on security, and revenue visibility, with a generally positive but probing tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, repeatedly referencing disciplined execution and strategic vision. In Q&A, the tone was steady, sometimes defensive in addressing adoption timelines and integration complexity, as in "It's early days" and "I think the opportunity is real."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident, while analysts became more focused on the execution challenges of large acquisitions and the translation of AI strategies into numbers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY 2026 revenue was raised to $11.28 billion to $11.31 billion, up from $10.50 billion to $10.54 billion previously. NGS ARR guidance moved to $8.52 billion to $8.62 billion from $7 billion to $7.1 billion. Operating margin guidance is slightly lower at 28.5% to 29% versus 29.5% to 30% prior.
  • Major acquisitions (CyberArk and Chronosphere) have closed and are integrated into guidance, compared to being pending last quarter. Increased detail was provided on integration strategy and expected synergies.
  • Platformization and SASE growth accelerated, with SASE ARR surpassing $1.5 billion. Prisma AIRS customer count more than tripled quarter-over-quarter.
  • Analysts shifted from broad questions on AI and growth to more detailed inquiries about integration, revenue attribution, and product adoption timing.
  • Management’s confidence was consistent, but focus shifted from announcing deals to executing integration and demonstrating early outcomes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged supply chain risks, noting "a marginal impact on product COGS this quarter from higher memory and storage pricing," but cited natural hedges and pricing actions.
  • Integration risks were addressed with detailed governance and onboarding strategies for Chronosphere and CyberArk.
  • Analysts questioned the pace of enterprise AI adoption and the lag in visible revenue, as well as potential distraction from large acquisitions.

Final Takeaway

Palo Alto Networks emphasized continued strong demand across its core cybersecurity businesses, underscored by record platformization wins, robust SASE and software firewall growth, and rapid adoption of AI security products like Prisma AIRS. The successful closure and integration of Chronosphere and CyberArk expand the company's reach into observability and identity, with management reiterating confidence in achieving $20 billion NGS ARR by 2030 and a 40% free cash flow margin by 2028. While integration and industry inflection risks remain, management stressed disciplined execution and ongoing innovation as critical to sustaining growth and leadership in the evolving AI-powered security landscape.
